critique.summary: The article explores the technical optimizations behind the 1999 game RollerCoaster Tycoon, highlighting its development almost entirely in Assembly language by Chris Sawyer. It discusses specific programming techniques and design decisions that enabled the game to run efficiently on late 1990s hardware, such as fine-grained data types, bit-shifting for math operations, and gameplay design choices that reduce computational load.
mainPoints: RollerCoaster Tycoon was primarily written in Assembly language, which contributed to its high performance on 1999 hardware.
The game uses fine-grained data types for money values to optimize memory and processing.
Bit-shifting operations replace some multiplication and division to improve speed.
Game design choices, such as guests wandering randomly instead of pathfinding to destinations, reduce expensive computations.
Pathfinding is limited in scope and selectively applied to avoid performance spikes.
Guests do not collide or avoid each other, allowing many to occupy the same space without costly calculations, though overcrowding affects guest happiness.
OpenRCT2, a fan-made reimplementation, provides insight into the original game's code and optimizations.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely accurate and consistent with known information about RollerCoaster Tycoon and its development. It is well-documented that Chris Sawyer wrote much of the game in Assembly, which was unusual for the time but contributed to its performance. The described optimizations, such as bit-shifting and selective pathfinding, are plausible and supported by the OpenRCT2 project's reverse engineering efforts. The article correctly notes that modern compilers reduce the performance gap between Assembly and high-level languages. The discussion about game design influencing performance is also credible and aligns with common practices in game development. However, the article is based on secondary sources and reverse engineering rather than direct original source code, which limits definitive verification.
considerations: The article relies heavily on the OpenRCT2 reverse engineering project rather than the original source code, which has not been publicly released.
It does not provide direct quotes or citations from Chris Sawyer or official development documentation.
The article focuses on technical and design optimizations but does not discuss other factors such as hardware constraints or marketing influences.
It assumes readers have some familiarity with programming concepts like Assembly and bit-shifting, which may limit accessibility.
The article does not compare RollerCoaster Tycoon’s optimization techniques with those of other contemporary games in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around technical aspects of game optimization, assembly programming, compiler behavior, and the interplay between technical constraints and game design. Participants share insights, personal experiences, and opinions on the importance of low-level coding and optimization strategies.

comments.author: netcoyote
comments.text:
Warcraft 1 (1994), Warcraft 2 (1995), and StarCraft (1998) all use power-of-2 aligned map sizes (64 blocks, 128 blocks, and 256 blocks) so the shift-factor could be pre-computed to avoid division/multiplication, which was dang slow on those old 386/486 computers.

Each map block was 2x2 cells, and each cell, 8x8 pixels. Made rendering background cells and fog-of-war overlays very straightforward assembly language.

All of Warcraft/etc. had only a few thousand lines of assembly language to render maps/sprites/fonts/fog-of-war into the offscreen buffer, and to blit from the offscreen buffer to the screen.

The rest of the code didn't need to be in assembly, which is too time-consuming to write for code where the performance doesn't matter. Everything else was written in portable assembler, by which I mean C.

Edit:

By way of comparison, Blackthorne for Super Nintendo was all 85816 assembly. The Genesis version (Motorola 68000) and DOS version (Intel 80386) were manually transcribed into their respective assembly languages.

The PC version of Blackthorne also had a lot of custom assembler macros to generate 100K of rendering code to do pixel-scrollable chunky-planar VGA mode X (written by Bryan Waters - https://www.mobygames.com/person/5641/bryan-waters/).

At Blizzard we learned from working on those console app ports that writing assembly code takes too much programmer time.

Edit 2:

I recall that Comanche: Maximum Overkill (1992, a voxel-based helicopter simulator) was written in all assembly in DOS real mode. A huge technical feat, but so much work to port to protected mode that I think they switched to polygon-rendering for later versions.
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed historical account of optimization techniques used in early RTS games, highlighting the importance of assembly for performance.

comments.author: youarentrightjr
comments.analysis: Corrects a common misconception about compiler optimizations, emphasizing that modern compilers handle such cases efficiently.

comments.author: Validark
comments.text:
"Since the number is stored in a binary system, every shift to the left means the number is doubled.

At first this sounds like a strange technical obscurity"

Do we not know binary in 2026? Why is this a surprise to the intended audience?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about the familiarity with binary operations among modern programmers, suggesting the topic's relevance has diminished.

comments.author: evandale
comments.text:
The pathfinding section reminded me that there's a YouTube steamer, Marcel Vos, who goes into a deep dive of how the pathfinding works.

https://youtu.be/twU1SsFP-bE

He has lots of videos that are deep dives into how RCT works and how things are implemented!
comments.analysis: Offers a helpful pointer to further technical content, enriching the discussion with external educational material.

comments.author: bze12
comments.text:
> This part is especially fascinating to me, since it turns an optimization done out of technical necessity into a gameplay feature.

Reminds me of blood moons in Zelda https://www.polygon.com/legend-zelda-tears-kingdom/23834440/...
comments.analysis: Insightfully connects technical constraints with creative game design, illustrating how limitations can inspire gameplay mechanics.

comments.author: raffraffraff
comments.text:
On huge games produced by large game studios, I wonder if the idea of using a real world technical challenge as a "feature" within the game is considered genius? Consider a coder and a game designer who are on different teams and don't attend the same meetings.

But if you look at creative writing, story arcs are all about obstacles. A boring story is made interesting by an obstacle. It is what our protagonist needs to overcome. A one-man-band game dev who simultaneously holds the story and the technical challenge their head, might spot the opportunity to use a glitch or limitation as, I dunno, a mini game that riffs on the glitch.
comments.analysis: Provides an interesting perspective on integrating technical limitations into game narrative and design, though more speculative.

comments.author: maxglute
comments.text: Is there a place to find stories of recent game optimization? What's most ridiculous on like quick inverse square route. As someone who spent way too much time vraying in prior life, I still can't believe we got real time ray tracing.
comments.analysis: Focuses on recent innovations without directly analyzing the article's content, offering a more general curiosity.

comments.author: makapuf
comments.text: Compilers won't do multiplication by power of two to bit shift for you ? I remember reading in ~2000: the only thing writing a<<2 instead of a/4 will do is make your compiler yawn
comments.analysis: Incorrectly asserts that modern compilers do not perform such optimizations, overlooking current compiler capabilities.

comments.author: troad
comments.text:
> When reading through OpenRCT2’s source, there is a common syntax that you rarely see in modern code, lines like this:

> NewValue = OldValue << 2;

I disagree with the framing of this section. Bit shifts are used all the time in low-level code. They're not just some archaic optimisation, they're also a natural way of working with binary data (aka all data on a computer). Modern low-level code continues to use lots of bit shifts, bitwise operators, etc.

Low-level programming is absolutely crucial to performant games. Even if you're not doing low-level programming yourself, you're almost certainly using an engine or library that uses it extensively. I'm surprised an article about optimisation in gaming, of all things, would take the somewhat tired "in ye olde days" angle on low-level code.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced view on low-level optimizations, correctly asserting their relevance in modern game development.
